title: The seasonal routine (ritucharya), simply
excerpt: Ayurveda has always held that nature shifts every two months, and your body should shift with it. Here is what the six-season framework of ritucharya actually means for your daily life.
body_html:
why you feel different every few months

You may have noticed that what feels good to eat in January, a bowl of rich dal with ghee and hot rotis, suddenly feels heavy and unpleasant by April. By June you want cool buttermilk instead. By October you are craving warm soups again. This is not a quirk of your mood. It is your body following a rhythm that Ayurveda mapped thousands of years ago.

That rhythm is called ritucharya, from ritu (season) and charya (conduct or regimen). It is the practice of adjusting your food, exercise, sleep, and daily habits as the seasons move through the year. The principle appears prominently in the Caraka Samhita (Sutrasthana) and the Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana, Ch. 3), both of which treat seasonal living as one of the foundational pillars of health, alongside daily routine and ethical conduct.

These are not exotic interventions. They are adjustments your grandparents probably made without naming them.

the six seasons and what shifts in each

The classical Indian year is divided into six seasons of roughly two months each, grouped into two large movements: the northern course (uttarayana), when the sun moves north and the environment becomes drier and more depleting, and the southern course (dakshinayana), when conditions become moister and more nourishing. The Ashtanga Hridaya (Sutrasthana) describes how strength, digestive capacity, and the balance of doshas all follow this arc.

Shishira (mid-January to mid-March, late winter): cold and dry. Vata and Kapha both accumulate. The body's digestive fire, agni, tends to be strong from the cold. Heavy, oily, sour, and salty foods are well-tolerated. Sesame, jaggery, urad dal, and warm milk with a little ginger are recommended. Physical exercise can be more vigorous at this time.

Vasanta (mid-March to mid-May, spring): the Kapha that built up over winter now begins to melt and release, often causing colds, congestion, and lethargy. Caraka Samhita (Sutrasthana) specifically mentions that heavy, oily foods should be reduced and lighter, bitter, and astringent tastes favoured. Barley, old wheat, honey, and warm herbal waters suit this season. This is traditionally a time for cleansing, including panchakarma for those who need it.

Grishma (mid-May to mid-July, summer): heat peaks, agni paradoxically weakens because the body redirects energy outward, and strength decreases. Cooling, light, liquid foods are emphasized: thin buttermilk, coconut water, rice kanji, and ripe fruits. Cold baths, light cotton clothes, and rest in shade are classically advised (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Heavy exercise is discouraged.

Varsha (mid-July to mid-September, monsoon): the rains bring humidity and erratic digestion. This is the season in which all three doshas are most easily disturbed. Digestive fire is at its most variable. The Sushruta Samhita advises favouring light, freshly cooked, warm food, avoiding raw salads and river water, and using digestive spices such as cumin, coriander, and a small amount of ginger. Medicated ghee is classically recommended in this season for its stabilising effect on agni.

Sharad (mid-September to mid-November, autumn): Pitta, which was suppressed during the rains, now flares as the sun re-emerges on a body that has grown accustomed to coolness. This is considered the season most prone to Pitta disorders, including skin flare-ups, acidity, and inflammatory conditions. Bitter, sweet, and astringent foods are recommended. Tikta ghrita (bitter ghee preparations) were traditionally given in this season. Moonlit nights, light cotton, and avoiding excessive sun are classic sharad advice (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana).

Hemanta (mid-November to mid-January, early winter): cold returns, agni strengthens again, and the body can handle richer nourishment. Milk, ghee, sesame, sugarcane preparations, and warming spices are all endorsed. This is considered the best season for building strength and nourishing tissues (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

simple ways to live with the seasons

You do not need to overhaul your life every two months. Ritucharya, at its most practical, is about small directional shifts, not rigid rules.

Adjust your cooking fat: ghee in winter, a lighter sesame or coconut oil in summer. Shift your grains: heavier preparations like thick khichdi in cold months, thinner rice or barley-based dishes in warm ones. Change your spice emphasis: warming spices like ginger and black pepper in winter, cooling digestives like coriander and fennel in summer and autumn.

Match your exercise to the season: full-effort workouts in winter and early spring, gentler walks and stretching in the heat of summer, moderate activity in autumn. Keep sleep slightly longer in winter and slightly shorter in summer, following the natural light as much as possible.

Drink water that matches the weather: warm in cold months, room-temperature in monsoon (avoid cold water from the fridge, which Ayurveda consistently associates with weakening digestion), and cool but not iced in summer. These are not exotic interventions. They are adjustments your grandparents probably made without naming them.

when to speak to a doctor or vaidya

Ritucharya is a framework for general wellbeing in healthy adults. It is not a treatment plan. If you experience significant seasonal symptoms such as severe respiratory infections each monsoon, intense skin flare-ups in autumn, or exhaustion that does not resolve with a change of season, please consult a qualified doctor or a trained Ayurvedic vaidya rather than trying to self-manage through diet alone.

In particular, see a doctor promptly if seasonal changes coincide with breathing difficulty, high fever, swollen joints, or marked changes in appetite and weight. This article is general health information and is not a substitute for personalised medical or Ayurvedic assessment.

Try This Today

  • Switch your cooking fat with the cold: Use ghee more freely from November through February when agni is strong and the body needs nourishment. Lighten the fat in May and June when digestion is naturally weaker.
  • Warm water through monsoon: From July to September, drink water that has been boiled and allowed to cool to room temperature rather than cold or chilled water. Add a small pinch of dry ginger if digestion feels sluggish.
  • Rest more in summer, move more in winter: In peak summer, keep exercise to an early-morning gentle walk. In winter and early spring, you can sustain longer, more vigorous sessions without depleting yourself.
  • Favour bitter and astringent in autumn: Through September and October, include bitter greens such as methi (fenugreek leaves) and karela (bitter gourd) in your meals a few times a week to help cool Pitta that tends to rise after the monsoon.
  • Eat seasonally local: The simplest ritucharya is to buy what is actually in season at your local market. Nature tends to produce what the body needs: cooling cucumbers and gourds in summer, warming root vegetables in winter.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Ginger (आर्द्रक / शुण्ठी)

Used as a warming digestive in winter and monsoon; a small pinch of dry ginger in warm water helps stabilise variable agni during the rains.

Cumin (जीरक)

A key digestive spice for monsoon cooking; helps kindle agni when humidity makes digestion sluggish.

Coriander (धान्यक)

A cooling digestive favoured in summer and autumn to balance Pitta without extinguishing digestive fire.

Fennel (मधुरिका)

Sweet and cooling; a gentle post-meal digestive suited to hot weather and autumn when Pitta is elevated.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Ayurveda has always held that nature shifts every two months, and your body should shift with it. Here is what the six-season framework of ritucharya actually means for your daily life.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Ayurveda divides the year into six two-month seasons, each requiring small shifts in food, exercise, and sleep.
Digestive strength (agni) rises in cold seasons and dips in heat and monsoon, so your meals should adjust accordingly.
Practical ritucharya is about directional tweaks, not rigid overhauls: adjust fat, grain, spice, and effort with the weather.

glossary_terms.definition: Seasonal regimen: ritu means season, charya means conduct or daily practice.
The digestive and metabolic fire that governs how well the body transforms food into nourishment.
The northern course of the sun (roughly January to July), associated with increasing dryness and a gradual decrease in bodily strength.
The southern course of the sun (roughly July to January), associated with cooling, moisture, and a gradual increase in nourishment and strength.
Ghee prepared with bitter herbs, classically prescribed in autumn to pacify Pitta that builds up during and after the rains.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Ritucharya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Ritucharya is defined as: Seasonal regimen: ritu means season, charya means conduct or daily practice.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to switch your cooking fat with the cold. Use ghee more freely from November through February when agni is strong and the body needs nourishment. Lighten the fat in May and June when digestion is naturally weaker.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Ginger is highly regarded. Used as a warming digestive in winter and monsoon; a small pinch of dry ginger in warm water helps stabilise variable agni during the rains.
